apple scab.symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots on leaves, becoming corky and cracked as they age. Causes premature fruit drop.
apple scab.causes: Ascomycete fungus overwintering in fallen leaves. Discharges spores during spring rains.
apple scab.spread: Wind and splashing rain water carry spores to emerging spring foliage.
organic_treatments: Apply liquid lime sulfur or sulfur sprays at green tip and petal fall stages.
Use certified organic copper octanoate sprays early in the season.
chemical_treatments: Apply captan, mancozeb, or chlorothalonil fungicides starting at bud break.
Use systemic triazole fungicides (e.g., myclobutanil) for post-infection control.
preventive_measures: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn to reduce overwintering spore loads.
Prune tree canopy to maximize sunlight penetration and speed up leaf drying.
apple scab.fertilizer_advice: Limit heavy nitrogen fertilizers in spring to avoid excessive lush foliage, which is highly susceptible to fungal infection.
apple scab.weather_sensitivity: Highly active during prolonged leaf wetness at moderate temperatures.
apple scab.recovery_guidance: Affected foliage will not heal, but preventive treatments protect new flush. Prune out dead twigs. Vigor recovery expected in 2-3 weeks.
apple scab.references: ICAR Horticulture Advisory Bulletin (2024), Cornell University Extension Publication #210

apple black rot.symptoms: Purple spots on leaves expanding into 'frogeye' lesions. Fruits develop dark, sunken rots with concentric rings.
apple black rot.causes: Fungal pathogen overwintering in mummified fruit, dead bark, and cankers.
apple black rot.spread: Rain splashes conidia from cankers and mummies onto leaves, twigs, and flowers.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper sprays before bloom.
Spray sulfur fungicides at weekly intervals during warm, wet periods.
chemical_treatments: Apply thiophanate-methyl or mancozeb at bud break.
Use strobilurin fungicides (e.g., pyraclostrobin) during vegetative growth.
preventive_measures: Prune out dead wood, cankers, and fire blight strikes during winter dormancy.
Remove and burn all mummified fruit hanging in trees or lying on the orchard floor.
apple black rot.fertilizer_advice: Ensure adequate potassium levels to promote bark healing and reduce canker susceptibility.
apple black rot.weather_sensitivity: Spreads rapidly during warm rain events. Spores germinate within 4 hours at optimal temperatures.
apple black rot.recovery_guidance: Prune out infected branches 6 inches below visible damage. Cankered trees may take a full season to recover.
apple black rot.references: FAO Plant Production Guidelines (2023), Penn State Extension Fruit Pathology Guide

apple cedar rust.symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces, developing tube-like structures (aecia) on the leaf underside.
apple cedar rust.causes: Heteroecious rust fungus requiring two hosts (apples and eastern red cedars) to complete its life cycle.
apple cedar rust.spread: Windborne basidiospores travel up to a mile from cedar galls to apple leaves during wet spring weather.
organic_treatments: Apply copper soap sprays starting at pink bud stage.
Apply sulfur dusts at weekly intervals during spring rain periods.
chemical_treatments: Apply myclobutanil or mancozeb starting at tight cluster stage.
Apply fenbuconazole at petal fall to block spore germination.
preventive_measures: Remove all eastern red cedar and juniper hosts within a 500-foot radius of the orchard.
Plant rust-resistant apple cultivars like Liberty, Freedom, or Enterprise.
apple cedar rust.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic compost to support root vigor. Avoid high-nitrogen spikes that induce susceptible soft shoots.
apple cedar rust.weather_sensitivity: Requires liquid moisture on apple leaves for at least 4-6 hours to initiate infection.
apple cedar rust.recovery_guidance: Defoliated trees recover slowly. Provide deep watering and humic acid to stimulate new root flushes. Vigor returns next spring.
apple cedar rust.references: University of Illinois Extension Fruit Report (2024), CABI Invasive Species Compendium

apple healthy.symptoms: Foliage is dark green, turgid, and free of lesions or velvety coatings. Vigor and shoot elongation are optimal.
organic_treatments: Maintain baseline organic mulch compost around base.
Spray seaweed extract foliar nutrients monthly to boost immunity.
chemical_treatments: None required. Maintain standard pre-scheduled protection plan.
preventive_measures: Perform routine visual inspections of lower canopy leaves.
Ensure drip irrigation lines are clear and watering is consistent.
apple healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply balanced organic NPK (5-5-5) at start of spring flush and post-harvest.
apple healthy.weather_sensitivity: Monitor for high-humidity pockets which may initiate disease cycles.
apple healthy.recovery_guidance: Continue current cultivation practices. Keep records of leaf flushes.

blueberry healthy.symptoms: Leaves are smooth, deep green, with no red spots, powdery margins, or leaf drop.
organic_treatments: Apply pine needle or pine bark mulch to preserve soil acidity.
Use kelp meal soil amendments for trace mineral supply.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Maintain soil pH between 4.5 and 5.2.
Perform annual winter pruning to remove old unproductive wood.
blueberry healthy.fertilizer_advice: Use ammonium sulfate or organic cotton seed meal to maintain acidic soil nitrogen levels.
blueberry healthy.weather_sensitivity: Ensure soil remains moist; blueberries have shallow roots sensitive to heat drought.
blueberry healthy.recovery_guidance: Optimal state. Continue regular soil pH checks.

cherry powdery mildew.symptoms: White, powdery patches of fungal growth on leaves and shoots. Infected leaves curl upward and drop prematurely.
cherry powdery mildew.causes: Obligate biotrophic fungus overwintering as chasmothecia in bark crevices and fallen leaves.
cherry powdery mildew.spread: Wind carries conidia from primary shoots onto expanding leaves during warm, dry days with high relative humidity at night.
organic_treatments: Spray potassium bicarbonate or horticultural oils.
Apply sulfur-based bio-fungicides at weekly intervals.
chemical_treatments: Apply quinoxyfen or myclobutanil at shuck split.
Use strobilurin class fungicides during active shoot growth.
preventive_measures: Prune internal branches to allow light penetration and air movement.
Avoid overhead irrigation to prevent high-humidity microclimates in the canopy.
cherry powdery mildew.fertilizer_advice: Reduce late-summer nitrogen applications to discourage soft, susceptible terminal growth.
cherry powdery mildew.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm days (20-25C) with dry leaf surfaces, coupled with high relative humidity at night.
cherry powdery mildew.recovery_guidance: Prune back severely distorted shoots. New leaves will grow healthy once humidity drops. Recovery in 2 weeks.
cherry powdery mildew.references: Oregon State University Extension Plant Disease Handbook (2024)

cherry healthy.symptoms: Leaves are vibrant green, flat, without powdery coatings, spots, or chewing damage.
organic_treatments: Apply well-rotted organic compost around the drip line.
Spray biological companion sprays to maintain leaf microbiomes.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Apply trunk wraps in winter to prevent southwest injury (sunscald).
Maintain uniform soil moisture during fruit enlargement.
cherry healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply balanced organic fruit tree fertilizer in early spring.
cherry healthy.weather_sensitivity: Watch for sudden spring frost which can destroy blooms.
cherry healthy.recovery_guidance: Excellent growth. Keep canopy pruned open.

corn gray leaf spot.symptoms: Rectangular, tan-to-gray lesions running parallel to leaf veins. Lesions block photosynthesis, leading to stalk lodging.
corn gray leaf spot.causes: Fungal pathogen overwintering in corn crop residue on the soil surface.
corn gray leaf spot.spread: Wind and splashing water carry spores from residue onto lower leaves in early summer.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper fungicides at early stages of leaf spotting.
Use biological bacillus subtilis sprays to inhibit spore growth.
chemical_treatments: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicides at VT (tasseling) stage.
Apply azoxystrobin-propiconazole blends if disease threshold exceeds 5% of leaf area.
preventive_measures: Rotate crops with non-hosts like soybean, alfalfa, or clover for 1-2 years.
Perform deep conventional tillage to bury infested corn residue.
corn gray leaf spot.fertilizer_advice: Maintain optimal potassium levels to increase stalk strength and reduce lodging risks.
corn gray leaf spot.weather_sensitivity: Thrives in warm, humid weather. Spores require 12 hours of leaf wetness to infect.
corn gray leaf spot.recovery_guidance: Fungicides arrest disease spread but do not heal existing lesions. Yield recovery depends on protecting leaves above the ear.
corn gray leaf spot.references: FAO Maize Disease Guide (2023), Iowa State University Crop Advisory

corn common rust.symptoms: Golden-brown to cinnamon-brown pustules on both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Pustules rupture to release powdery spores.
corn common rust.causes: Fungus migrating northward via wind currents from southern overwintering regions; does not overwinter in cold northern residues.
corn common rust.spread: Windborne urediniospores travel long distances and settle on wet corn leaves.
organic_treatments: Spray sulfur dusts or organic copper soaps during early infection.
Use potassium bicarbonate foliar sprays.
chemical_treatments: Apply pyraclostrobin or tebuconazole if pustules appear before tasseling.
Apply propiconazole at first sign of infection on seed corn.
preventive_measures: Plant commercial hybrids bred with specific resistance (Rp genes).
Plant early in the season to avoid peak spore migration windows.
corn common rust.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced nitrogen levels; excessive nitrogen promotes soft leaf tissue that rust spores easily penetrate.
corn common rust.weather_sensitivity: Active in cool, moist, and cloudy weather conditions. Free water on leaf is required.
corn common rust.recovery_guidance: High recovery rate if treated before widespread pustule rupture. Leaf integrity recovers in 10-14 days.
corn common rust.references: ICAR Field Crop Pathology Manual (2024), Purdue Extension Corn Guide

corn northern leaf blight.symptoms: Long, cigar-shaped, grayish-green lesions (1 to 6 inches long). Leaves look burned or frosted.
corn northern leaf blight.causes: Ascomycete fungus overwintering as chlamydospores in crop residue.
corn northern leaf blight.spread: Spores are carried by wind or rain splashes onto new corn leaves during warm, wet periods.
organic_treatments: Apply copper octanoate foliar treatments at V6-V8 stages.
Spray compost tea or bio-fungicides to suppress spore activity.
chemical_treatments: Apply strobilurin fungicides at VT (tasseling) stage.
Apply benzovindiflupyr or prothioconazole blends for high-risk fields.
preventive_measures: Select hybrids with Ht genes for resistance.
Rotate corn with soybeans or small grains to allow residue decomposition.
corn northern leaf blight.fertilizer_advice: Ensure adequate phosphorus levels to encourage early root establishment and overall crop vigor.
corn northern leaf blight.weather_sensitivity: Favored by moderate temperatures and prolonged humid wetness (dew or light rain).
corn northern leaf blight.recovery_guidance: Fungicide spray must occur before 50% canopy coverage is affected. Ear fill recovers if sprayed early.
corn northern leaf blight.references: FAO Grain Advisory Bulletin (2024), University of Minnesota Extension

corn healthy.symptoms: Leaves are erect, deep green, with no cigar-shaped lesions, pustules, or leaf margins showing yellowing.
organic_treatments: Apply blood meal or fish emulsion nitrogen during knee-high stage.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Ensure uniform seed planting depth for even emergence.
Monitor drainage systems to prevent waterlogging around roots.
corn healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply side-dress nitrogen (urea or anhydrous ammonia) at V4-V6 growth stages.
corn healthy.weather_sensitivity: Monitor heat index; corn requires deep moisture during pollination.

grape black rot.symptoms: Small, round, tan spots on leaves with dark borders. Berries turn red-brown, shrivel, and dry into hard black mummies.
grape black rot.causes: Fungus overwintering in mummified berries on the vine or on the ground.
grape black rot.spread: Rain drops splash spores from overwintering berries onto new shoots and fruit clusters.
organic_treatments: Apply copper fungicides during early spring pre-bloom.
Spray sulfur fungicides at weekly intervals until berry touch.
chemical_treatments: Apply mancozeb or captan starting at 1-inch shoot growth.
Use DMI class fungicides (e.g., tebuconazole) post-bloom for fruit protection.
preventive_measures: Prune out and destroy all mummified grape clusters and dead wood during winter.
Keep canopy well-trained to vertical trellises to maximize wind drying.
grape black rot.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic compost around vine roots. Avoid nitrogen over-fertilization which produces dense, damp canopies.
grape black rot.weather_sensitivity: Requires constant leaf wetness for 6-24 hours depending on temperature.
grape black rot.recovery_guidance: Shriveled berries cannot recover. Prune out damaged clusters immediately. Focus on protecting remaining green berries.
grape black rot.references: FAO Viticulture Technical Guidelines (2024), Ohio State Extension Grape Guide

grape black measles.symptoms: Interveinal leaf yellowing and drying (tiger-stripe pattern). Berries show small, round, dark spots.
grape black measles.causes: Complex of wood-rotting fungi invading pruning wounds on older vines.
grape black measles.spread: Wind and rain disperse fungal spores onto fresh pruning cuts during winter and early spring.
organic_treatments: Apply organic pruning wound protectants (e.g., copper paste) immediately after cutting.
Use biological Trichoderma-based wound seals.
chemical_treatments: None available for cured vines. Treat pruning wounds with thiophanate-methyl sprays.
Drench trunk wounds with standard fungicides in late winter.
preventive_measures: Prune vines during dry weather in late winter; avoid pruning during or immediately after rain.
Paint pruning cuts larger than 0.5 inches with specialized sealants.
grape black measles.fertilizer_advice: Ensure balanced nutrition and avoid water stress to reduce vascular strain on infected vines.
grape black measles.weather_sensitivity: Symptoms are expressed severely during hot, dry summer spells when water demand is high.
grape black measles.recovery_guidance: Chronic wood disease. Prune back trunks to healthy wood. Vines may take years to re-establish canopy.
grape black measles.references: University of California Davis IPM Grape Reports (2023)

grape leaf blight.symptoms: Dull red to brown spots on leaves, expanding to cover large portions of the blade. Leaves dry and drop.
grape leaf blight.causes: Fungal pathogen overwintering on fallen leaves and dead canopy wood.
grape leaf blight.spread: Spores are splashed by rain onto lower canopy leaves, moving upward as the season progresses.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper octanoate sprays at first sign of leaf spots.
Use sulfur or bacillus amyloliquefaciens foliar treatments.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb at pre-bloom stages.
Use strobilurin class fungicides if wet weather persists after bloom.
preventive_measures: Ensure lower leaves are pruned to prevent contact with wet soil.
Mow orchard floor grasses short to increase air flow beneath the vines.
grape leaf blight.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic compost and humic acid to build root capacity and leaf vigor.
grape leaf blight.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm, humid weather with frequent rains.
grape leaf blight.recovery_guidance: Defoliated vines produce weak buds next year. Protect remaining foliage with copper. Recovery takes 3-4 weeks.

grape healthy.symptoms: Leaves are wide, deep green, free of spots, necrosis, or curling. Vine tendrils are climbing actively.
organic_treatments: Maintain cover crop (clover or vetch) between rows to balance soil biology.
Apply organic mulch around vine bases.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Prune vines to a dual-arm Kniffin or cordon system to maximize air penetration.
Maintain drip irrigation schedules during berry swelling.
grape healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply potassium sulfate during fruit-set to support sugar translocation.
grape healthy.weather_sensitivity: Watch for sudden heavy rain during harvest, which can split ripe berries.

orange huanglongbing.symptoms: Asymmetrical leaf mottling (blotchy mottle). Fruits are small, lopsided, remain green at the bottom, and taste bitter.
orange huanglongbing.spread: Transmitted by the Asian Citrus Psyllid (Diaphorina citri) during feeding on young flush.
organic_treatments: Apply organic horticultural oils to suppress Asian Citrus Psyllid vector populations.
Release predatory wasps (Tamarixia radiata) as a biological control agent.
chemical_treatments: Apply systemic neonicotinoid insecticides (e.g., imidacloprid) to young trees.
Spray pyrethroid or organophosphate insecticides during flush periods to kill adult vectors.
preventive_measures: Immediately remove and burn infected trees to eliminate inoculum source.
Plant only certified disease-free citrus trees grown in insect-proof screenhouses.
orange huanglongbing.fertilizer_advice: Provide enhanced nutritional foliar programs (micronutrients zinc, manganese, boron) to support productivity of infected trees.
orange huanglongbing.weather_sensitivity: Psyllid vectors are highly active in warm, dry weather during new leaf flush.
orange huanglongbing.recovery_guidance: Fatal bacterial disease. There is no cure. Trees slowly decline and die within 3-5 years. Removal is mandatory to save orchard.
orange huanglongbing.references: FAO Citrus Greening Manual (2024), University of Florida Citrus Research Center

peach bacterial spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked, purple-to-black spots on leaves, often dry and drop out leaving a 'shot-hole' appearance.
peach bacterial spot.causes: Bacterium overwintering in twig cankers and buds.
peach bacterial spot.spread: Wind-blown rain splashes bacteria from cankers onto emerging leaves and developing fruit in spring.
organic_treatments: Apply copper sprays at dormant and bud swell stages.
Apply biological Bacillus subtilis sprays during the growing season.
chemical_treatments: Apply oxytetracycline (antibiotic) starting at petal fall at weekly intervals.
Use zinc-based bactericides during late spring cover sprays.
preventive_measures: Plant resistant cultivars (e.g., Redhaven, Loring, Cresthaven).
Avoid planting orchards in highly sandy soils which exacerbate plant stress and disease susceptibility.
peach bacterial spot.fertilizer_advice: Apply moderate nitrogen levels to maintain vigor, but avoid over-application which creates dense, humid canopy cover.
peach bacterial spot.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm, wet spring weather with high winds.
peach bacterial spot.recovery_guidance: Prune out twig cankers. Shot-holed leaves will function partially but expect minor leaf drop. Vigor returns in 3-4 weeks.
peach bacterial spot.references: FAO Peach Disease Technical Guide (2023), Clemson University Extension Fruit Reports

peach healthy.symptoms: Leaves are lanceolate, light-to-mid green, free of shot-hole spots, curling, or leaf curl puckering.
organic_treatments: Apply organic bark mulch to retain root moisture.
Spray fish emulsion fertilizer to support shoot growth.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Perform regular pruning to maintain open-center shape (V-shape canopy).
Thin young fruits in spring to prevent branch breakage and tree stress.
peach healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply balanced fruit tree fertilizer after harvest in late summer.
peach healthy.weather_sensitivity: Peach trees are sensitive to late spring freeze events.

pepper bell bacterial spot.symptoms: Small, dark, circular spots on leaves, becoming raised and scabby on the underside. Leaves turn yellow and drop.
pepper bell bacterial spot.causes: Bacterium overwintering on crop residue, weed hosts, and seeds.
pepper bell bacterial spot.spread: Rain splash, overhead irrigation, and working with wet plants spread bacteria.
organic_treatments: Spray organic copper octanoate at 7-10 day intervals.
Use biological competitor sprays (e.g. Bacillus subtilis).
chemical_treatments: Apply streptomycin sulfate sprays at early seedling stage.
Apply copper-mancozeb tank mixes during humid periods.
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free seeds and transplants.
Rotate peppers with non-solanaceous crops for at least 2-3 years.
pepper bell bacterial spot.fertilizer_advice: Ensure adequate calcium and potassium to enhance leaf cell wall integrity.
pepper bell bacterial spot.weather_sensitivity: Active in warm, wet weather. Drops leaves rapidly if humidity remains high.
pepper bell bacterial spot.recovery_guidance: Defoliated plants will have lower yields. Protect new growth with copper. Recovery seen in 2-3 weeks.
pepper bell bacterial spot.references: FAO Vegetable Pathology Circular (2024), University of Florida IFAS Extension

pepper bell healthy.symptoms: Leaves are broad, shiny, dark green, free of puckering, spots, or yellowing edges.
organic_treatments: Mulch bed with clean straw to suppress weeds and retain water.
Drench roots with compost tea weekly.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Use drip irrigation to keep leaf surfaces dry.
Maintain spacing of 18 inches between plants to allow wind movement.
pepper bell healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic high-nitrogen fertilizer before flowering, switching to high-potassium during fruit set.
pepper bell healthy.weather_sensitivity: Pepper plants shed flowers if temperature exceeds 35C.
pepper bell healthy.recovery_guidance: Vigorous growth. Fruit development is healthy.

potato early blight.symptoms: Dark brown, circular spots with concentric target-board rings on older leaves. Lower leaves yellow and die.
potato early blight.causes: Fungus overwintering in soil, crop debris, and volunteer potato plants.
potato early blight.spread: Wind and splashing rain disperse conidia onto lower canopy leaves.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper fungicides at first sign of target spots.
Spray sulfur dusts or bio-fungicides to inhibit spore spread.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin.
Spray boscalid or difenoconazole in rotation to prevent chemical resistance.
preventive_measures: Practice a 3-year crop rotation; avoid planting tomatoes or eggplants in rotation.
Destroy volunteer potato plants and nightshade weeds around field borders.
potato early blight.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced nitrogen nutrition; nitrogen-deficient plants are highly susceptible to early blight.
potato early blight.weather_sensitivity: Favored by alternating wet and dry canopy conditions (heavy dew followed by hot dry days).
potato early blight.recovery_guidance: Fungicide arrests progress. Keep lower leaves trimmed to stop upward spore movement. Recovery expected in 2 weeks.
potato early blight.references: ICAR Tuber Pathology Manual (2024), University of Idaho Extension Potato Guide

potato late blight.symptoms: Water-soaked, dark green to black lesions starting at leaf tips. White mildew growth appears on leaf under-surface during wet weather.
potato late blight.causes: Oomycete pathogen overwintering in seed tubers and cull piles.
potato late blight.spread: Cool wind currents carry sporangia for miles; splashing rain spreads zoospores locally.
organic_treatments: Apply copper sulfate or copper hydroxide immediately at low-humidity periods.
Spray biologicals like Bacillus subtilis to suppress oomycete germination.
chemical_treatments: Apply metalaxyl, chlorothalonil, or fluazinam.
Spray systemic propamocarb or cyazofamid under high infection risk.
preventive_measures: Plant only certified, disease-free seed tubers.
Destroy all cull piles (dumped waste potatoes) by burying or freezing.
potato late blight.fertilizer_advice: Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization which delays tuber maturity and creates dense, damp crop canopies.
potato late blight.weather_sensitivity: Requires cool temperatures (15-20C) and high humidity (fog, rain, heavy dew) for spore production.
potato late blight.recovery_guidance: Highly destructive. If infection exceeds 50%, destroy crop to prevent tuber rot. Vigor recovery only possible if sprayed at trace levels.
potato late blight.references: FAO Global Late Blight Advisory (2024), Irish Agriculture Advisory Board Bulletins

potato healthy.symptoms: Leaves are broad, flat, green, free of concentric target spots or dark water-soaked margins.
organic_treatments: Hill soil around plant base to protect developing tubers from sun exposure.
Spray compost tea to maintain leaf cuticle defenses.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Maintain uniform soil moisture; avoid dry-to-wet soil swings which split tubers.
Inspect lower canopy leaves weekly.
potato healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply high-phosphorus organic fertilizer during planting, adding potassium during hilling.
potato healthy.weather_sensitivity: Monitor for heavy rain which can wash away hilled soil.
potato healthy.recovery_guidance: Excellent growth. Ensure plants are hilled.

raspberry healthy.symptoms: Leaves are lobed, bright green, free of gray mold, rust pustules, or leaf margins showing yellowing.
organic_treatments: Apply organic bark mulch to protect shallow root systems.
Spray liquid seaweed extract monthly.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Prune canes annually; remove spent floricanes after summer harvest.
Ensure trellis support keeps canes off the ground.
raspberry healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic compost or well-rotted manure around base in late winter.
raspberry healthy.weather_sensitivity: Monitor relative humidity; raspberry fruit mold thrives in stagnant air.
raspberry healthy.recovery_guidance: Vigorous canopy. Trellis supports are clear.

soybean healthy.symptoms: Leaves are trifoliate, uniform green, free of rust pustules, downy mildew coatings, or leaf spot lesions.
organic_treatments: None required.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Ensure spacing allows air movement between rows.
Monitor fields for bean leaf beetle vector populations.
soybean healthy.fertilizer_advice: None needed if soil is inoculated with Rhizobium bacteria; soybeans fix their own nitrogen.
soybean healthy.weather_sensitivity: Requires consistent soil moisture during pod filling stage.
soybean healthy.recovery_guidance: Healthy growth. Nodes are forming normally.

squash powdery mildew.symptoms: Talcum-powder-like white spots on leaves and stems. Leaves turn brown, shrivel, and dry up.
squash powdery mildew.causes: Fungus overwintering on weed hosts and crop debris.
squash powdery mildew.spread: Windborne conidia travel long distances; humidity favors spore development.
organic_treatments: Spray potassium bicarbonate or neem oil.
Apply milk-water spray mixtures (1:9 ratio) under bright sunlight.
chemical_treatments: Apply myclobutanil or triadimefon.
Use strobilurin class fungicides (e.g. trifloxystrobin) in rotation.
preventive_measures: Plant resistant squash varieties.
Provide wide spacing (3 feet) between plants to increase air movement.
squash powdery mildew.fertilizer_advice: Avoid nitrogen over-application; excess nitrogen induces soft, susceptible leaves.
squash powdery mildew.weather_sensitivity: Thrives in warm, dry day conditions with high relative humidity at night.
squash powdery mildew.recovery_guidance: Infected leaves will remain scarred. Cut out dead vines to allow new, healthy leaves to grow. Vigor returns in 10 days.
squash powdery mildew.references: FAO Cucurbit Manual (2024), Clemson Extension vegetable guides

strawberry leaf scorch.symptoms: Numerous small, purplish spots on leaves. Leaf margins curl upward, brown, and dry up, looking 'scorched'.
strawberry leaf scorch.causes: Fungal pathogen overwintering on dead leaves.
strawberry leaf scorch.spread: Rain drops splash conidia from old debris onto newly emerged leaves in spring.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper fungicides at early leaf emergence.
Spray biologicals to populate leaf surfaces and outcompete spores.
chemical_treatments: Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl pre-bloom.
Apply dodine or iprodione if spotting is observed in runner plants.
preventive_measures: Rake and remove all crop debris at renovation after harvest.
Maintain weed control around beds to improve low-canopy wind flow.
strawberry leaf scorch.fertilizer_advice: Apply nitrogen fertilizers only in late summer after harvest; spring applications increase scorch susceptibility.
strawberry leaf scorch.weather_sensitivity: Active during prolonged leaf wetness at moderate to warm temperatures.
strawberry leaf scorch.recovery_guidance: Remove scorched leaves manually to clean the canopy. Vigor recovery expected in 2 weeks.
strawberry leaf scorch.references: USDA Small Fruits Technical Circular (2024), Ohio State Extension

strawberry healthy.symptoms: Leaves are dark green, trifoliate, without purplish lesions, margins are flat and turgid.
organic_treatments: Mulch beds with pine needles or clean straw to keep berries off damp soil.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Use drip irrigation to prevent crown rot.
Plant in raised beds to ensure rapid root drainage.
strawberry healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic bone meal during early spring bud break.
strawberry healthy.weather_sensitivity: Sensitive to waterlogging; roots rot in soggy soils.
strawberry healthy.recovery_guidance: Optimal state. Straw mulches are clean.

tomato bacterial spot.symptoms: Small, dark, greasy spots on leaves with yellow halos. Spots dry out and the center collapses. Leaves drop.
tomato bacterial spot.causes: Bacterial pathogen carried on seeds, stakes, or crop residues.
tomato bacterial spot.spread: Rain splash, high humidity, overhead irrigation, and handling wet plants.
organic_treatments: Spray organic copper fungicides at 7-10 day intervals.
Use biological sprays containing Bacillus subtilis.
chemical_treatments: Apply streptomycin sulfate at seedling stages.
Apply copper-mancozeb tank mixes.
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free seeds and transplants.
Sanitize stakes, cages, and tools with disinfectant annually.
Rotate tomatoes with corn or beans; avoid peppers/potatoes.
tomato bacterial spot.fertilizer_advice: Ensure adequate potassium levels to support leaf structure and disease resistance.
tomato bacterial spot.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm (24-32C), humid weather with frequent rainfall.
tomato bacterial spot.recovery_guidance: Bacterial infections are systemic; fungicides only check spread. Remove severely defoliated plants. Vigor return takes 3 weeks.
tomato bacterial spot.references: ICAR Solanaceous Pathology Reports (2024), Florida Extension Tomato Guide

tomato early blight.symptoms: Brown, circular spots with concentric target-board rings on lower leaves. Leaves yellow and fall off.
tomato early blight.causes: Fungus overwintering in soil and crop residue.
tomato early blight.spread: Wind and splashing rain water carry conidia onto lower leaves.
organic_treatments: Apply copper octanoate or copper hydroxide sprays.
Spray organic biological competitor products.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb at V4-V6 stages.
Use systemic pyraclostrobin if rain patterns persist.
preventive_measures: Stake and prune lower leaves up to 12 inches from soil.
Apply organic straw mulch around the base of plants.
tomato early blight.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced nitrogen-potassium ratios; weak, nitrogen-starved tomatoes are highly susceptible.
tomato early blight.weather_sensitivity: Favored by warm temperatures and frequent dew or light rains.
tomato early blight.recovery_guidance: Prune off spotted lower leaves immediately. Protect remaining canopy. Recovery expected in 10-14 days.
tomato early blight.references: FAO Vegetable Technical circular (2023), Cornell Extension Tomato Guide

tomato late blight.symptoms: Large, irregular water-soaked dark lesions on leaves and stems. White, fuzzy fungal growth on the underside during humid mornings.
tomato late blight.causes: Oomycete pathogen overwintering in live potato tubers and solanaceous weeds.
tomato late blight.spread: Wind currents carry sporangia for miles; splashing water spreads zoospores.
organic_treatments: Apply copper hydroxide immediately at low-humidity periods.
Use biological bacillus subtilis sprays to inhibit zoospore germination.
chemical_treatments: Apply metalaxyl, chlorothalonil, or cyazofamid.
Apply systemic propamocarb for high-risk field containment.
preventive_measures: Keep canopy pruned to maximize air movement.
Destroy all volunteer tomatoes, potatoes, and nightshade weeds near fields.
tomato late blight.fertilizer_advice: Avoid heavy nitrogen fertilizers which stimulate dense leaf growth and trap humidity.
tomato late blight.weather_sensitivity: Active during cool (15-20C), wet, foggy, and highly humid weather.
tomato late blight.recovery_guidance: Extremely destructive. Pull and burn infected plants instantly to save neighboring beds. Vigor recovery is highly unlikely if stem lesions are present.
tomato late blight.references: ICAR Solanaceous Advisory (2024), Cornell University Extension

tomato leaf mold.symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces. Olive-green velvety fungal growth on the leaf underside.
tomato leaf mold.causes: Fungus overwintering in seed, crop residue, and greenhouse structures.
tomato leaf mold.spread: Conidia carried by wind, rain, tools, and clothing.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper octanoate sprays.
Spray sulfur dust or bio-fungicide formulations.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb.
Use DMI class systemic fungicides (e.g. difenoconazole).
preventive_measures: Increase greenhouse ventilation; keep relative humidity below 85%.
Prune lower foliage and stake tomatoes to prevent leaf overlapping.
tomato leaf mold.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced potassium levels to increase cell wall rigidity.
tomato leaf mold.weather_sensitivity: Active in high relative humidity (above 85%) and moderate temperatures.
tomato leaf mold.recovery_guidance: Improve airflow immediately. Velvety spots will remain but spread will halt. Canopy recovery expected in 2 weeks.
tomato leaf mold.references: Greenhouse Vegetable Disease Compendium (2023), Penn State Extension

tomato septoria leaf spot.symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots on lower leaves with dark borders and gray centers. Center of spots shows tiny black pycnidia.
tomato septoria leaf spot.causes: Fungus overwintering in crop debris, solanaceous weeds, and wooden stakes.
tomato septoria leaf spot.spread: Spattered water drops splash conidia from soil surface onto foliage.
organic_treatments: Apply copper hydroxide or organic copper soaps.
Spray biological sprays at weekly intervals.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb.
Spray azoxystrobin if disease is progressing upward.
preventive_measures: Apply 2 inches of organic mulch (straw or plastic) to block soil splash.
Clean all stakes and cages with bleach or alcohol at season end.
tomato septoria leaf spot.fertilizer_advice: Incorporate potassium and organic compost to support canopy density.
tomato septoria leaf spot.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm (20-25C), wet weather with high rainfall.
tomato septoria leaf spot.recovery_guidance: Cut off heavily spotted lower leaves. Protect new leaves with copper. Recovery expected in 10-14 days.
tomato septoria leaf spot.references: ICAR Tomato Advisory Bulletin (2024), Ohio State Extension

tomato spider mites.symptoms: Fine yellow-to-white stippling (specks) on leaves. Leaves look dusty and turn bronze. Fine webbing on leaf under-surfaces.
tomato spider mites.causes: Acarid pest multiplying rapidly in hot, dry conditions.
tomato spider mites.spread: Mites crawl locally or crawl onto silk threads to blow on the wind.
organic_treatments: Apply organic neem oil, insecticidal soap, or horticultural oils.
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) into the canopy.
chemical_treatments: Apply abamectin or bifenazate miticides.
Spray spiromesifen under high infestation pressures.
preventive_measures: Keep field borders clear of dusty weeds which harbor mites.
Ensure plants receive adequate moisture; drought-stressed tomatoes release stress volatiles that attract mites.
tomato spider mites.fertilizer_advice: Avoid over-application of nitrogen; excess nitrogen increases amino acid levels in leaves, making them highly nutritious for mites.
tomato spider mites.weather_sensitivity: Pest population explodes in hot, dry weather with low relative humidity.
tomato spider mites.recovery_guidance: Mites cause permanent leaf scarring. Wash leaves with water pressure to break webs. New shoots emerge healthy in 10 days.
tomato spider mites.references: FAO Tomato Pest Management Guides (2024), University of California IPM

tomato target spot.symptoms: Zonate, brown spots on leaves with dark margins. Fruits develop sunken, dark brown, circular lesions.
tomato target spot.causes: Fungus overwintering in soil, weeds, and crop residues.
tomato target spot.spread: Windblown spores and splashing rain drops disperse conidia.
organic_treatments: Apply organic copper fungicides.
Use Bacillus subtilis based bio-fungicides.
chemical_treatments: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or strobilurin fungicides.
Use penthiopyrad or fluxapyroxad in rotation.
preventive_measures: Use drip irrigation to keep leaf canopy dry.
Maintain crop spacing of 24 inches to allow wind drying.
tomato target spot.fertilizer_advice: Maintain balanced organic NPK nutrition to prevent premature plant senescence.
tomato target spot.weather_sensitivity: Active during warm, highly humid periods with long leaf wetness duration.
tomato target spot.recovery_guidance: Affected fruits are unmarketable. Protect remaining foliage with copper. Recovery seen in 2 weeks.
tomato target spot.references: FAO Vegetable Advisory (2024), Florida Tomato Institute Reports

tomato yellow leaf curl.symptoms: Leaves are small, curl upward and inward, and show severe interveinal yellowing (chlorosis). Plants are stunted.
tomato yellow leaf curl.spread: Transmitted by the Sweetpotato Whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) during sap feeding.
organic_treatments: Spray organic insecticidal soaps or neem oil to suppress whiteflies.
Apply reflective plastic mulch to deter whiteflies from landing.
chemical_treatments: Use systemic neonicotinoid insecticides (e.g., imidacloprid) in seedling soil.
Apply spiromesifen or cyantraniliprole to control whitefly nymphs and adults.
preventive_measures: Install 50-mesh whitefly-proof netting on greenhouse windows.
Rake and remove infected plants immediately to prevent virus reservoir build-up.
tomato yellow leaf curl.fertilizer_advice: Apply foliar seaweed extracts to help stunted plants absorb micro-nutrients.
tomato yellow leaf curl.weather_sensitivity: Whitefly vector populations swell rapidly in dry, warm summer months.
tomato yellow leaf curl.recovery_guidance: Systemic viral infection. Affected plants will not recover and fruit set stops. Destroy infected plants immediately.
tomato yellow leaf curl.references: ICAR Virology Bulletins (2024), University of Florida Extension

tomato mosaic virus.symptoms: Mottling of leaves (alternating light and dark green areas). Leaves can look narrow, puckered, or fern-like.
tomato mosaic virus.spread: Extremely stable virus spread mechanically via hands, tools, stakes, and plant-to-plant contact.
organic_treatments: Wash hands and tools with dry milk powder or soap solution before handling plants.
Remove infected plants immediately.
chemical_treatments: None available. Disinfect tools with trisodium phosphate (TSP) or virucidal sanitizers.
preventive_measures: Use certified seed; treat seeds with hot water or trisodium phosphate.
Remove nightshade weeds and ground cherries near the field.
tomato mosaic virus.fertilizer_advice: Maintain baseline organic NPK compost. Avoid stress that triggers symptom expression.
tomato mosaic virus.weather_sensitivity: Symptoms are expressed severely in moderate temperatures and overcast sky conditions.
tomato mosaic virus.recovery_guidance: Highly contagious. No chemical cure. Pull and bury plants immediately. Disinfect stakes and cages.
tomato mosaic virus.references: FAO Virology Manual (2023), Cornell Extension Tomato Reports

tomato healthy.symptoms: Leaves are broad, dark green, flat, without greasy yellow spots, target rings, curling, or webbing.
organic_treatments: Apply organic compost or worm castings around base.
Stake plants to keep fruits off soil.
chemical_treatments: None required.
preventive_measures: Maintain consistent watering at root zone to prevent blossom-end rot.
Stake and trellis vines for maximum airflow.
tomato healthy.fertilizer_advice: Apply organic high-phosphorus/calcium fertilizer during bloom, adding nitrogen after fruit set.
tomato healthy.weather_sensitivity: Requires consistent watering; sudden moisture swings split tomatoes.
tomato healthy.recovery_guidance: Vigorous growth. Fruit development is clean.
